kept.text:
$IONQ 

Synopsys researchers published a paper on arXiv today. They ran an electromagnetic field simulation on IonQ's quantum hardware. The paper claims this is the first quantum-hardware demonstration of its kind.

Maxwell's equations describe how electromagnetic fields move through space. Chip designers, antenna designers, radar systems, and photonic components inside data centers all depend on them.

Synopsys sells software that runs these simulations. Their customers come from automotive, aerospace, defense, telecommunications, and semiconductor industries.

Tuesday I posted about Roetteler's work with Synopsys. That work ran structural mechanical simulations on IonQ hardware. A Rolls-Royce jet engine model ran 15 percent faster. The technique was optimization, which finds the best answer out of many possible options.

Today's paper uses a different technique called Hamiltonian simulation. It uses a quantum computer to predict how something physical changes over time, when you already know the physics rules. Engineers use it to simulate how electromagnetic waves travel and how molecules behave. The computer follows the physics step by step.

Synopsys researchers are now publishing work on IonQ hardware under their own names.

The paper is a research demonstration, not a production deployment. The hardware showed device noise. The grid was small at 16 by 16. The original quantum program was too long to run, so the researchers shrank it by 200 times before the IonQ machine could execute it. None of these are surprising for a first demonstration.

The hardware named is IonQ Forte Enterprise. The customer is publishing research on IonQ hardware, this time for electromagnetic simulation rather than structural mechanics.

This is what it looks like when one customer keeps finding new uses for IonQ.

https://t.co/E2zI9NrwGR

profile_bio.description: Tracking IonQ where quantum, AI, and runtime become behavior.
